assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

NOTE: Ideally, you should have a notebook that is at least 8 x 10 inches, so you have room to draw your own maps and also glue in maps that are printed off.

➜ INTRO
This year, we are learning about mapmaking and global exploration. You will be learning to draw your own maps as well. You may want to first practice outlining the map on a whiteboard or another piece of paper before putting it in your notebook. As time permits on drawing days, you are encouraged to use watercolors or colored pencils to fill in your outlines (see the link for examples done by students). Your Geography notebook will be used on both days of the week. Make sure you label each entry with the date or lesson number. There will be days when you will be prompted to follow the instructions for an activity in the Activity Section of your Mapping the World with Art book.
∞ Image Link: Watercolor Map Examples

➜ READ
Reading 1: The Very Earliest Maps

➜ NARRATE & NOTEBOOK
1\. Narrate orally what you remember about the reading.
2\. Then, in your notebook, include some kind of narration. You can look back at the text as needed. You can write a short paragraph, write bullet points, or draw a concept map or pictures with captions. You can be as creative as you like. We provided examples of other students' work. Typically, each two-page spread in your notebook will have a narration on one side and your map drawings on the other. There may be weeks in which you will have two drawings per two-page spread (especially in Term 3).
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 1
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 2
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 3

➜ DRAW
p.1-2 Map Drawing 1 Mesopotamia (use the book or video option)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading. For example, under the map of Mesopotamia, you could say, "The earliest maps were drawn in clay by the people of Mesopotamia."

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Mesopotamia as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Mesopotamia

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

What did you read last time about the very earliest maps?

➜ READ

2\. In your notebook, include some kind of narration. You can write a short paragraph, write bullet points, or draw diagrams or pictures with captions.

➜ DRAW
p.3-4 Map Drawing 2 Nile River (Use the book or video option)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading. This map pertains to the first reading. You might have to look back to remember what was said about Egypt and the earliest maps.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the Nile River as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of the Nile River

• EXTRA HELPING
Check out this video for a deeper look at the history of cartography.
∞ Video Link: Origin of the World Map

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Mapping the World with Art
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Mapping the World with Art: Tell about Marco Polo and why he was important to the history of maps. OR Tell about Henry the Navigator and his contributions to map-making.

· Mapping the World with Art: Draw a map of one of the countries you learned about this term and label anything you can remember. Suggestions include the following: Italy, Greece, the Iberian Peninsula, Britain, Israel, India, Africa, and South America.

assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook, parchment paper, copy of pattern on p.129, pen(s), color pencils.

PREP: Make sure you have the materials needed for the map drawing activity. You may purchase parchment paper for printing from Amazon or Staples. However, if you would like to age your paper, take a look at the video link. We suggest you make the aged paper a day before you are going to use it. This will give the paper time to dry for use in the lesson.
∞ Video Link: How To Make Paper Look Old

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
p.128 Activity Section: Map review (this will take you two weeks to complete, especially if you take your time and add color to your map).
Activity Idea 30C: Make an antique map of the Eastern Hemisphere. Follow the instructions in the book. This will be a good review of the locations you have already drawn in the Eastern Hemisphere.

assignment_description:
John Audubon: Beloved Boy
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, World Atlas (optional), notebook, and map links

➜PREP
In your notebook, you should set aside the last 5 pages for this term's day 1 assignment. Label the section "Audubon’s Wilderness Travels." You will also need a blank 1850’s United States map to mark the places mentioned in your readings. 
∞Map Link: 1850s U.S. Map

➜INTRODUCTION
1. In this term, we will have follow the life of the naturalist John James Audubon as he explores the wilderness of America during the late 1800s, a time when the new frontier still had much to learn about the land’s wildlife. 
2. You may want to review vocabulary as needed.

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Materials: United States Atlas p.27, World Atlas or world map link
∞Map Link: World Map
1\. On the world map link or in a Student World Atlas (p.30-31) , can you locate Haiti and France? The island of Haiti has two main languages: Haitian Creole and French. Due to its history of French colonization, the island still speaks and uses French in formal settings. The majority of people, however, speak Haitian Creole (French-influenced). 
2\. Now look at p.27 in your United States Atlas. Locate Pennsylvania. All these places were significant to John James Audubon. He was born in Haiti, raised in France, and then came to Pennsylvania when he was a young man.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
This Strange Wilderness Prologue-Ch.1 p.xv-7 “In the heat” - ”a great wilderness.”

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook, 1850s U.S. map
Print the 1850s map. Cut along the border of the page. Tape or paste this page on your first blank page in the “Audubon’s Wilderness Travels” section.  You will be tracking the places he visits. Label this map, "Audubon's Travels".

• DEFINITIONS
observation: careful watching to collect information

naturalist: someone who studies plants and animals in nature

medium: the material used by an artist (e.g., watercolor)

field notes: short written records made during observation

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW
p.61-63 Map Drawing 26A Australia, 26B New Zealand

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Australia and New Zealand as you draw yours. Learn more about the region.
∞ Map Link: Map of Australia
∞ Map Link: Map of New Zealand
∞ Video Link: Why New Zealand Isn't Part of Australia?

Parents - make a note to review "Prep" for Lesson 66. This will give you time to gather the materials you will need for this term's final "Mapping the World with Art" project.

John Audubon: America My Country
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.47
Locate Philadelphia. How would you describe its location?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.2 p.9-16 “Audubon sat in” - ”on the frontier.”

➜ VIEW
This is an engraving of a flatboat from the 1800s similar to what Audubon would gave traveled down the Ohio River in.
∞ Image Link: Flatboat of 1800s

Look at either the painting of the Eastern Phoebe on p. 8 or the Wild Turkey on p. 14. Similar to picture study, describe what you see—colors, textures, background, foreground, etc.

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Using colored markers to outline the borders, outline the state of Pennsylvania on your map. Write Pennsylvania inside the borders. Use a light shade of colored pencils to shade in the state.

• DEFINITIONS
émigré / immigrant: a person who moves from one country to another

plantation: a large farm where crops and, previously, enslaved labor was used

apprentice: a learner who trains under an expert

• EXTRA HELPING
You may want to watch part of this video to learn more about the painting of Wild Turkey.
∞ Video Link: Audubon's Wild Turkey (ends at 4:12)

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW
p.64-65 Map Drawing 27A Alaska

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Alaska as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Alaska

John Audubon: The American Woodsmen
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.66-67
1\. Locate the state capital city of Kentucky. What is it? (Frankfort)
2\. Along the Indiana border, can you locate the city of Henderson? You will find the John James Audubon State Park in Henderson, Kentucky.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.3 p.17-27 “Owning a store” - ”had never existed.”

➜ VIEW
Watch the video for a short tour of John James Audubon State Park in Henderson, Kentucky.
∞ Video Link: John James Audubon State Park

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Using colored markers to outline the borders, outline the state of Kentucky on your map. Write Kentucky inside the borders. Use a different light shade of colored pencils to shade in the state.

• DEFINITIONS
commerce: buying and selling goods (Audubon also worked in business.)

natural history: the study of animals, plants, and the environment

• EXTRA HELPING
In this 5-minute video, you can learn more about the painting of The Trumpeter Swan and why the version in the book is not the finished one.
∞ Video Link: Audubon's Trumpeter Swan (Watch 8:18-14:34)

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW
p.66-69 Map Drawing 27B Kamchatka Peninsula and the Sea of Okhotsk

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the Kamchatka Peninsula & the Sea of Okhotsk as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Kamchatka Peninsula & the Sea of Okhotsk

John Audubon: Down the Mississippi
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, map link, notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Materials: Map Link
Locate the Mississippi River. The river is the second-longest river in the United States, flowing about 2,350 miles. Observe the map link. Do you recognize the rivers from Term 1 and Term 2? Which ones?
∞ Map Link: Mississippi River Map

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness
Ch.4 p.29-36 “When Audubon reached” - ”met Mrs. Pirrie.”

➜ VIEW
Pick one of the illustrations in the chapter to observe and describe what you see—colors, textures, background, foreground, etc.

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Draw the Mississippi River on the map located in your notebook. Feel free to attempt to recreate a bird picture in your notebook.

• DEFINITIONS
riverine: related to rivers

navigation: moving along waterways

delta: where a river meets the sea and deposits sediment

migration: seasonal movement of animals (especially birds)

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW
p.70-76 Map Drawing 28A The rest of North America

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of North America as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of North America

John Audubon: On The Wing
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.68-69
Locate New Orleans on the map. Notice how the Mississippi runs through this area and will continue about 100 miles downstream to empty at the Gulf of Mexico.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.5 p.39-48 “Lucretia Pirrie was” - ”for Liverpool, England.”

➜ VIEW
Pick one of the illustrations in the chapter to observe and describe what you see—colors, textures, background, foreground, etc.

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Using colored markers to outline the borders, outline the state of Louisiana on your map. Write Louisiana inside the borders. Use a different light shade of colored pencils to shade in the state. Feel free to attempt to recreate a bird picture.

• DEFINITIONS
plumage: a bird’s feathers

wingbeat: the movement of a bird’s wings while flying

anatomy: the structure of an organism

field sketch: a quick drawing made outdoors to record movement or shape

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Take out one of your blank maps of Canada and label as many places as you remember from the provinces and territories studied thus far. Then compare your work to the Atlas or your notebook.

➜ DRAW
p.77-78 Map Drawing 28B The top of Asia

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the top of Asia as you draw yours.

John Audubon: Birds of America
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, Student World Atlas, notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Materials: Student World Atlas, p.82-83
∞ Map Link: World Map
1\. Locate London on p.83. Which countries are part of the United Kingdom? (England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland.)
2\. Locate France on p.83. Looking at p. 82—which body of water separates the UK from France? (The English Channel, or as it is called in France, “la Manche.”)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.6 p.49-60 “Two months at” - ”home a star.”

➜ VIEW
If time permits, watch the following video:
∞ Video Link: Engraving of Audubon Prints

• DEFINITIONS
folio: a very large book format (used for Audubon’s plates)

subscriber: someone who pays in advance to receive installments of a publication

engraving: a method of printing images from metal plates

portfolio: a collected set of works

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

p.79-81 Map Drawing 29A New Britain, New Ireland, and the Solomon Islands

➜ VIEW

John Audubon: This Strange Wilderness
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.84-85
Explore the map of the Midwest region of the United States. Locate the Mississippi River, the Missouri River, and the Platte River. Now take a look at the states that today make up the Great Plains area (North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, and Kansas). During Audubon’s exploration, this area was still only a territory and not divided into states.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.8 p.74-80 “From its earliest” - ”should we live?”

➜ VIEW
Pick one of the illustrations in the chapter to observe and describe what you see—colors, textures, background, foreground, etc.

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Use a different color to highlight and shade the Great Plains area in the map in your notebook (1850s map).

Final Project: Create a World Map
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, materials will vary depending on which option you choose. Check your book p.136, for the various possibilities.

PREP: Parents, this should be a final project that requires at least 4 lessons to complete (including this lesson). Make sure to read through the instructions before beginning. Once you and your student have decided which option they will work on, begin to gather the materials needed. Have a designated space where they can put their final project away safely to be worked on the next time. Maps are not perfect; have the student plan out their project in quadrants to ease the process. Ideally, they should be working from memory as best they can. The book recommends that you help them include a few reference points before they begin, such as longitude and/or latitude lines like the Equator, Prime Meridian, or Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn, to name a few. (Light lines for these points; they are mere references for their map drawings.)

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
p.135 Activity Section: Final Project: Creating a World Map
Create a World Map following the instructions in your book, starting on p.135. Take four weeks to complete, working one quadrant each week.
Have fun!

John Audubon: The Strange Wilderness, Then and Now
⍞ Materials: This Strange Wilderness, United States Atlas, notebook

➜ RECAP    
What did you read last time?

➜ LOCATE   
⍞ Map Resource: United States Atlas p.15, 115
1\. Locate Texas on the map. What states border Texas? (Louisiana, Arkansas, Oklahoma, New Mexico) 
2\. Locate Missouri on the map. What states border Missouri? (Arkansas, Oklahoma, Kansas, Nebraska, Iowa, Illinois, Kentucky, and Tennessee)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
This Strange Wilderness Ch.8-9 p.80-90 “All we cared” - ”nature around me.”

➜MAP WORK
⍞ Materials: notebook
Using colored markers to outline the borders, outline the states of Texas and Missouri on your map. Write their names inside the borders. Use a different light shade of colored pencils to shade in each state. How colorful is your map? Are you impressed with all the places Audubon traveled to?

➜ WRITE
On the last page in your Audubon section, write a letter to Audubon reflecting on how you feel about his contribution to ornithology and geography.

• DEFINITIONS
legacy: something left behind for future generations

conservationist: a person working to protect nature

ethics: principles about right and wrong (introduce briefly: collecting vs. modern non‑lethal study)

citizen science: community volunteers contributing to scientific data (e.g., bird counts)

Final Project: Create a World Map (Cont.)

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geography: Grade 7 (U.S.) — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· This Strange Wilderness: Describe how Audubon's passion for birds was influential in exploring the American Wilderness. Be sure to use a specific example of a location he experienced.

· Mapping the World with Art: Draw a map of one of the countries you learned about this term and label anything you can remember. Suggestions include the following: Australia, New Zealand, Alaska, North America, and Antarctica.
